questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and let $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3) \rbrace$ on $A$. Is $R$ reflexive on $A$?
options: Yes, because every element $a \in A$ has $(a,a) \in R$
No, because $R$ contains no pair $(a,b)$ with $a \neq b$
Yes, but only because $R$ has exactly three pairs
questions.explanation: Reflexivity on $A$ requires $(a,a) \in R$ for every $a \in A$. Here $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R$ contains $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$, and $(3,3)$, so all three loops are present and $R$ is reflexive. Reflexivity says nothing about off-diagonal pairs, so the absence of pairs like $(1,2)$ is irrelevant, and symmetry is a separate property entirely. The trap is thinking a relation must relate distinct elements to count as reflexive, when in fact reflexivity is only about the diagonal pairs $(a,a)$.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R = \lbrace (1,2), (2,1), (1,3), (3,1) \rbrace$ on $A$. Is $R$ symmetric?
options: Yes, because whenever $(a,b) \in R$ the pair $(b,a) \in R$ as well
Yes, because $R$ contains no pair of the form $(a,a)$
questions.explanation: Symmetry requires that whenever $(a,b) \in R$, the reversed pair $(b,a)$ is also in $R$. Checking each pair: $(1,2)$ has its partner $(2,1)$, and $(1,3)$ has its partner $(3,1)$, so every pair is matched and $R$ is symmetric. There is no obligation to include $(2,3)$, since $R$ never contained $(3,2)$ either, and reflexivity is a different requirement. The trap is confusing symmetry (reverse every pair you have) with completeness or reflexivity: a symmetric relation need not relate every element, only mirror the pairs it does contain.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Consider the relation $f = \lbrace (1,a), (2,b), (3,a) \rbrace$ from $X = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ to $Y = \lbrace a, b, c \rbrace$. Is $f$ a function from $X$ to $Y$?
options: No, because the element $c$ of $Y$ is never used
No, because $a$ appears as an output twice
Yes, because each element of $X$ is assigned exactly one element of $Y$
No, because $X$ and $Y$ have the same number of elements
questions.explanation: A relation from $X$ to $Y$ is a function when every element of the domain $X$ is paired with exactly one element of $Y$. Each of $1$, $2$, and $3$ appears as a first coordinate exactly once, mapping to $a$, $b$, and $a$ respectively, so $f$ is a function. A codomain value being unused (here $c$) or an output being repeated (here $a$) are both allowed for functions. The trap is thinking a function must hit every codomain value or must never repeat an output: those would describe surjectivity and injectivity, not the bare definition of a function.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $g = \lbrace (1,a), (1,b), (2,c) \rbrace$ be a relation from $X = \lbrace 1, 2 \rbrace$ to $Y = \lbrace a, b, c \rbrace$. Is $g$ a function from $X$ to $Y$?
options: Yes, because all three pairs are distinct
Yes, because both elements of $X$ appear as inputs
No, because $a$ and $b$ are different outputs
No, because the input $1$ is paired with two different outputs
questions.explanation: For $g$ to be a function, each input may be paired with only one output. But the input $1$ appears in both $(1,a)$ and $(1,b)$, so $1$ is sent to two different outputs and the single-valued requirement fails. It does not matter that the pairs are distinct or that every input is used; one input with two outputs already breaks it. The trap is calling a relation a function just because it lists tidy distinct pairs: the test is whether any single input maps to more than one output.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : A \to B$ where $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$, $B = \lbrace p, q, r, s \rbrace$, and $f = \lbrace (1,p), (2,q), (3,p) \rbrace$. What is the range of $f$?
questions.explanation: The range is the set of values actually produced, that is, the second coordinates that really occur. The outputs are $p$, $q$, and $p$ again, so the range is $\lbrace p, q \rbrace$. The full set $\lbrace p, q, r, s \rbrace$ is the codomain $B$, which includes values like $r$ and $s$ that $f$ never reaches, and $\lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ is the domain. The trap is reporting the codomain in place of the range: the codomain is where outputs are allowed to live, while the range is only the values that are actually hit.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Define $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ by $f(x) = 2x + 5$. Which best describes $f$?
questions.explanation: Suppose $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$, so $2x_1 + 5 = 2x_2 + 5$; subtracting $5$ and dividing by $2$ gives $x_1 = x_2$, so $f$ is injective. For any target $y \in \mathbb{R}$, solving $2x + 5 = y$ gives $x = (y-5)/2 \in \mathbb{R}$, so every real is hit and $f$ is surjective. Being both, $f$ is bijective. The trap is assuming a non-constant linear map on $\mathbb{R}$ might miss some outputs: a line with nonzero slope covers all of $\mathbb{R}$, so it is onto as well as one-to-one.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R = \lbrace (1,2), (2,3) \rbrace$ on $A$. Is $R$ transitive?
options: Yes, because $R$ has only two pairs
No, because $(1,3)$ is required but missing
Yes, because $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$ point in the same direction
questions.explanation: Transitivity demands that whenever $(a,b) \in R$ and $(b,c) \in R$, the pair $(a,c)$ is in $R$ too. Here $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$ form a chain through $2$, so transitivity forces $(1,3) \in R$, but $(1,3)$ is absent, so $R$ is not transitive. Having few pairs does not excuse a relation; even one broken chain is enough to fail. The trap is declaring a relation transitive without testing every chain $a \to b \to c$: the pair $(1,2),(2,3)$ creates an obligation that must be checked.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (2,1) \rbrace$ on $A$. Which list of properties does $R$ satisfy?
options: Symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
Reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
questions.explanation: All loops $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$, $(3,3)$ are present, so $R$ is reflexive. The only off-diagonal pairs are $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$, which mirror each other, so $R$ is symmetric. For transitivity, the chains using $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ give $(1,1)$ and $(2,2)$, both present, and no other chains lead anywhere new, so $R$ is transitive. It is therefore an equivalence relation, with classes $\lbrace 1, 2 \rbrace$ and $\lbrace 3 \rbrace$. The trap is stopping at reflexive and symmetric and assuming transitivity must fail: here the short chains all close up, so it holds.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2) \rbrace$ on $A$. Which statement is correct?
questions.explanation: Symmetry would need $(2,1)$ to accompany $(1,2)$, but $(2,1) \notin R$, so $R$ is not symmetric. Antisymmetry asks that if both $(a,b)$ and $(b,a)$ are in $R$ then $a = b$; the only two-way pairs are the diagonal loops, where indeed $a = b$, and $(1,2)$ has no reverse to worry about, so antisymmetry holds. Hence $R$ is antisymmetric but not symmetric. The trap is treating symmetric and antisymmetric as opposites: they are different conditions, and a relation can satisfy one, the other, both, or neither.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4 \rbrace$ consider $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4), (1,3), (3,1), (2,4), (4,2) \rbrace$. The equivalence classes of $R$ are:
questions.explanation: First confirm $R$ is an equivalence relation: every diagonal loop is present (reflexive), each off-diagonal pair has its reverse (symmetric), and the chains $1 \to 3 \to 1$ and $2 \to 4 \to 2$ close up (transitive). The class of $1$ is everything related to $1$, namely $\lbrace 1, 3 \rbrace$, and the class of $2$ is $\lbrace 2, 4 \rbrace$. These two classes partition $A$. The trap is grouping by numerical closeness, giving $\lbrace 1,2 \rbrace$ and $\lbrace 3,4 \rbrace$: classes are determined by which elements $R$ actually relates, not by which numbers look adjacent.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Define a relation on $\mathbb{Z}$ by $a \sim b$ exactly when $a - b$ is even. Which property does $\sim$ FAIL to have?
options: Antisymmetry only, which equivalence relations need not have
questions.explanation: For any integer $a$, $a - a = 0$ is even, so $\sim$ is reflexive. If $a - b$ is even then $b - a = -(a-b)$ is even, so $\sim$ is symmetric. If $a - b$ and $b - c$ are both even, their sum $a - c$ is even, so $\sim$ is transitive. All three hold, so $\sim$ is an equivalence relation and fails none of them; its classes are the even and odd integers. The trap is hunting for a missing property out of habit: this congruence-mod-$2$ relation genuinely satisfies all three, so the correct answer is that nothing fails.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace \to \lbrace a, b, c \rbrace$ be given by $f = \lbrace (1,b), (2,c), (3,a) \rbrace$. Which best describes $f$?
questions.explanation: The three inputs map to three distinct outputs $b$, $c$, $a$, so no two inputs share an image and $f$ is injective. Those outputs are exactly the three codomain elements $a$, $b$, $c$, so every codomain value is hit and $f$ is surjective. A function between finite sets of equal size that is injective is automatically surjective here, so $f$ is bijective. The trap is assuming a reordering like this could only be one-to-one: because it uses up all three codomain values, it is onto as well, hence a bijection.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4 \rbrace \to \lbrace a, b, c \rbrace$ with $f = \lbrace (1,a), (2,b), (3,c), (4,b) \rbrace$. Which best describes $f$?
questions.explanation: The outputs are $a$, $b$, $c$, $b$, so all three codomain values appear and $f$ is surjective (onto). But $2$ and $4$ both map to $b$, so two distinct inputs share an image and $f$ is not injective. Hence $f$ is surjective but not one-to-one. The trap is swapping the two notions: hitting every codomain value is surjectivity, while distinct inputs giving distinct outputs is injectivity, and a repeated output like $b$ breaks injectivity, not surjectivity.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $f(x) = 2x + 1$ and $g(x) = x^2$ be functions from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$. What is $(f \circ g)(3)$, that is, $f(g(3))$?
questions.explanation: By definition $(f \circ g)(3) = f(g(3))$, so apply $g$ first: $g(3) = 3^2 = 9$. Then apply $f$ to that result: $f(9) = 2 \cdot 9 + 1 = 19$. The value $49 = (2 \cdot 3 + 1)^2$ comes from doing the maps in the wrong order, computing $g(f(3))$ instead. The trap is reversing the composition: in $f \circ g$ the function nearest the input, namely $g$, acts first, and $f$ acts on its output.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ let $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (2,3), (1,3) \rbrace$. Is $R$ a partial order on $A$?
options: Yes: it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
No, because it is not reflexive
No, because it is not antisymmetric
No, because it is not transitive
questions.explanation: All loops are present, so $R$ is reflexive. No off-diagonal pair has its reverse (there is no $(2,1)$, $(3,2)$, or $(3,1)$), so antisymmetry holds vacuously for the distinct pairs. For transitivity, $(1,2)$ with $(2,3)$ needs $(1,3)$, which is present, and the other chains lead to pairs already in $R$. So $R$ is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive, hence a partial order (it is the usual $\le$ on $\lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$). The trap is doubting transitivity: the one chain that matters, $1 \to 2 \to 3$, is closed by the pair $(1,3)$.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $R$ be the divisibility relation on $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 6 \rbrace$, where $a\,R\,b$ means $a$ divides $b$. In the Hasse diagram of $R$, which element is the greatest (the top)?
questions.explanation: Divisibility is a partial order, and the greatest element is the one that every element divides, sitting at the top of the Hasse diagram. Since $1, 2, 3$, and $6$ all divide $6$, the element $6$ is above everything and is the greatest element. The element $1$ divides everything, but that makes it the least element (the bottom), not the greatest. The trap is reading the Hasse diagram upside down: under divisibility the top is the common multiple $6$, while the universal divisor $1$ is the bottom.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2) \rbrace$. Why is $R$ NOT an equivalence relation?
options: It is not reflexive, since $(3,3) \notin R$
It is not transitive, since $(1,3) \notin R$
It has too many pairs to be an equivalence relation
questions.explanation: Check the three conditions. All three loops $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$, $(3,3)$ are present, so $R$ is reflexive, and every pair appears with its reverse, so $R$ is symmetric. Transitivity fails: $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$ are both in $R$, which would force $(1,3)$, but $(1,3) \notin R$, so $R$ is not transitive and cannot be an equivalence relation. The trap is declaring a relation an equivalence relation after checking only reflexivity and symmetry: transitivity must be checked along every chain, and the missing link $(1,3)$ breaks it here.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be defined by $f(x) = x^2$. Which best describes $f$?
questions.explanation: Injectivity fails because $f(-2) = 4 = f(2)$ while $-2 \neq 2$, so two different inputs share an image. Surjectivity fails because no real $x$ gives $f(x) = -1$; squares are never negative, so the negative reals in the codomain are never reached. Failing both, $f$ is neither injective nor surjective. The trap is granting one of the two properties on reflex: on all of $\mathbb{R}$, squaring collapses $\pm x$ together (not one-to-one) and skips the negatives (not onto), so it has neither property.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace \to \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ be the bijection $f = \lbrace (1,2), (2,3), (3,1) \rbrace$. What is its inverse $f^{-1}$?
options: $f$ has no inverse because it is not injective
questions.explanation: Since $f$ is a bijection it has an inverse, obtained by reversing every ordered pair. Reversing $(1,2), (2,3), (3,1)$ gives $(2,1), (3,2), (1,3)$, which sorted by first coordinate is $\lbrace (1,3), (2,1), (3,2) \rbrace$. This $f^{-1}$ undoes $f$: for instance $f(1) = 2$ and $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. The trap is leaving the pairs unreversed (option one repeats $f$ itself) or assuming no inverse exists: $f$ is a bijection, so the inverse does exist and is found by flipping each pair.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ and $R = \lbrace (1,2), (2,1), (1,3) \rbrace$. Which single property does $R$ have?
options: Symmetric? No. Transitive? No. Reflexive? No. (none of the three)
questions.explanation: Reflexivity fails because no loops $(a,a)$ appear at all. Symmetry fails because $(1,3) \in R$ but $(3,1) \notin R$. Transitivity fails because $(2,1)$ and $(1,3)$ form a chain requiring $(2,3)$, which is absent; the chain $(1,2),(2,1)$ also requires $(1,1)$, again absent. So $R$ has none of the three properties. The trap is seeing the mirrored pair $(1,2),(2,1)$ and calling the whole relation symmetric: the lone pair $(1,3)$ has no partner, so symmetry fails, and the unchecked chains break transitivity too.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $f(x) = x + 3$ and $g(x) = 2x$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Which formula gives the composite $(g \circ f)(x) = g(f(x))$?
questions.explanation: In $g \circ f$ the inner function $f$ acts first: $f(x) = x + 3$. Then $g$ is applied to that whole result: $g(x+3) = 2(x+3) = 2x + 6$. The answer $2x + 3$ comes from computing $f(g(x)) = 2x + 3$ instead, reversing the order, and $2x + 6$ is the correct $g$-after-$f$ value. The trap is reversing the order of composition: $g \circ f$ means do $f$ first then $g$, so $g$ must be applied to the entire expression $x + 3$, distributing the factor of $2$ across both terms.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$, which of these relations is a partial order?
questions.explanation: A partial order must be reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. The second relation has all three loops (reflexive), no reversed off-diagonal pair (antisymmetric), and its only chain $1 \to 2 \to 3$ is closed by $(1,3)$ (transitive), so it qualifies. The first fails antisymmetry because $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ both appear yet $1 \neq 2$; the third has no loops, so it is not reflexive; the fourth both misses $(3,3)$ and contains the symmetric pair $(1,2),(2,1)$. The trap is accepting a relation with a two-way pair like $(1,2),(2,1)$ as an order: that mirrored pair destroys antisymmetry.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ be $f(n) = 2n$. Which statement is correct?
options: $f$ is injective but not surjective onto $\mathbb{Z}$
questions.explanation: If $f(m) = f(n)$ then $2m = 2n$, so $m = n$, and $f$ is injective. But the codomain is all of $\mathbb{Z}$, and an odd integer such as $3$ is never of the form $2n$ for integer $n$, so $f$ is not surjective onto $\mathbb{Z}$. Hence $f$ is injective but not surjective, and so not bijective. The trap is assuming an injective map between copies of $\mathbb{Z}$ must also be onto: over the integers, doubling reaches only the even numbers, leaving every odd target unhit.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $f : \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace \to \lbrace a, b \rbrace$ be $f = \lbrace (1,a), (2,b), (3,a) \rbrace$. Does $f^{-1}$ exist as a function from $\lbrace a, b \rbrace$ to $\lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$?
options: No, because $f$ is not injective, so no inverse function exists
questions.explanation: An inverse function exists only when $f$ is a bijection, in particular injective. Here $f(1) = a$ and $f(3) = a$, so two inputs share the output $a$ and $f$ is not injective. Reversing the pairs would give $(a,1)$ and $(a,3)$, which assigns the input $a$ two different outputs and so is not even a function. Surjectivity alone is not enough. The trap is claiming an inverse for a non-injective function: without one-to-one behavior the reversed relation is multivalued, so $f^{-1}$ fails to be a function.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ let $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2) \rbrace$. Which property does $R$ lack, keeping it from being an equivalence relation?
options: Transitivity, since $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$ are in $R$ but $(1,3)$ is not
It lacks no property; $R$ is an equivalence relation
questions.explanation: Every loop is present, so $R$ is reflexive, and each off-diagonal pair has its mirror, so $R$ is symmetric. Transitivity fails: $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$ chain through $2$ and demand $(1,3)$, but $(1,3) \notin R$ (and symmetrically $(3,1)$ is missing too). So $R$ is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive, hence not an equivalence relation. The trap is concluding an equivalence relation from reflexivity and symmetry alone: transitivity must be checked through the linking element, and the chain $1 \to 2 \to 3$ is exactly what breaks here.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $f(x) = x + 1$ and $g(x) = x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$. For which value of $x$ does $(f \circ g)(x) = (g \circ f)(x)$?
questions.explanation: Compute each composite carefully. $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = x^2 + 1$, since $g$ acts first then $f$ adds $1$. $(g \circ f)(x) = g(f(x)) = (x+1)^2 = x^2 + 2x + 1$, since $f$ acts first then $g$ squares. Setting them equal, $x^2 + 1 = x^2 + 2x + 1$ gives $2x = 0$, so $x = 0$. The trap is treating composition as commutative: in general $f \circ g \neq g \circ f$, and forcing the two orders to agree here pins down the single solution $x = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$, how many relations on $A$ are both reflexive and symmetric?
questions.explanation: A relation on $A$ is a subset of the $9$ ordered pairs in $A \times A$. Reflexivity forces the $3$ diagonal pairs $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)$ to be present, so they are not free choices. The $6$ off-diagonal pairs split into $3$ mirror couples, $\lbrace (1,2),(2,1) \rbrace$, $\lbrace (1,3),(3,1) \rbrace$, $\lbrace (2,3),(3,2) \rbrace$, and symmetry means each couple is either wholly in or wholly out: $3$ independent binary choices give $2^3 = 8$. The trap is counting all $6$ off-diagonal pairs as free ($2^6 = 64$): symmetry ties each pair to its mirror, halving the exponent to $3$.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: Let $f = \lbrace (1,4), (2,5), (3,4) \rbrace$ from $\lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ to $\lbrace 4, 5, 6 \rbrace$ and $g = \lbrace (4,a), (5,b), (6,c) \rbrace$ from $\lbrace 4, 5, 6 \rbrace$ to $\lbrace a, b, c \rbrace$. What is $g \circ f$?
questions.explanation: For $g \circ f$, send each input through $f$ first, then through $g$. Input $1$: $f(1) = 4$, then $g(4) = a$, giving $(1,a)$. Input $2$: $f(2) = 5$, then $g(5) = b$, giving $(2,b)$. Input $3$: $f(3) = 4$, then $g(4) = a$, giving $(3,a)$. So $g \circ f = \lbrace (1,a), (2,b), (3,a) \rbrace$. The trap is matching inputs to outputs positionally and writing $(3,c)$: you must actually follow $3 \mapsto 4 \mapsto a$, and since $f(3) = 4$ (not $6$), the value $c$ is never reached.
questions.source: in the style of Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ consider $R = \lbrace (1,2), (2,3) \rbrace$. What is the smallest relation containing $R$ that is transitive (its transitive closure)?
questions.explanation: Transitivity requires that the chain $(1,2)$ with $(2,3)$ forces $(1,3)$ to be present, so we must add $(1,3)$. After adding it, check for new chains: $(1,3)$ pairs with nothing starting at $3$, and no other chains arise, so no further pairs are needed. The smallest transitive relation containing $R$ is therefore $\lbrace (1,2), (2,3), (1,3) \rbrace$. The trap is over-adding the diagonal loops or reversed pairs: the transitive closure adds only the pairs that chains genuinely force, here just $(1,3)$, and does not throw in reflexive or symmetric pairs.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications

questions.stem: On $A = \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4 \rbrace$, the relation $R = \lbrace (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4), (1,2), (2,1) \rbrace$ is an equivalence relation. Which collection is its set of equivalence classes (the induced partition)?
questions.explanation: Each equivalence class is the set of elements related to a given element. Since $1$ and $2$ are related to each other (and to themselves), they form one class $\lbrace 1, 2 \rbrace$. The elements $3$ and $4$ are related only to themselves, so each sits alone, giving $\lbrace 3 \rbrace$ and $\lbrace 4 \rbrace$. The partition is thus $\lbrace \lbrace 1, 2 \rbrace, \lbrace 3 \rbrace, \lbrace 4 \rbrace \rbrace$. The trap is lumping $3$ and $4$ together: $R$ never relates $3$ to $4$, so they belong to separate singleton classes rather than a shared block.
questions.source: in the style of Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications
